2: The world demand for animal products is increasing in response to growing population and income, especially in developing countries (Gerber et al., 2013). In particular, poultry meat repre- sents one of the most consumed animal food products in the world and the world poultry population is predicted to rise by over 90% between 2005 and 2050 (Alexandratos and Bruinsma, 2012).

The livestock sector significantly contributes to anthropogenic environmental impact through the emission of pollutants and greenhouse gases into soil, water and air and through the con- sumption of non-renewable and limited resources, such as water and land, mainly for the production of animal feeds.
In terms of greenhouse gas emission, animal productions are responsible for 14.5% of human-induced Global Warming Potential (GWP), expressed as CO2 equivalents; but poultry meat and egg

productions, despite their importance, contribute for just 8% to the livestock sector emissions (Gerber et al., 2013). Poultry meat, in fact, is one of the most environmentally efficient productions in com- parison to other animal meat products (Gerber et al., 2013; Nijdam et al., 2012; Williams et al., 2006), mainly due to the excellent feed conversion of poultry.

In 2014 in Italy approximately 1,261,000 t of poultry carcass weight was produced, over 69% of which represented by broiler meat (AVEC, 2015). With these figures, Italy is largely self-sufficient (107%) with regard to the supply of poultry meat. Most of the farms raising broilers are located in the Northern regions of Italy (Veneto, Emilia-Romagna and Lombardia) and are characterized by modern strategies of management and high production intensity (NOMISMA, 2016).

Most of the Italian poultry production (96%, according to NOMISMA, 2016) is characterized by a completely vertically inte- grated production system that includes all stages of the production process, from feed manufacturing to the delivery of poultry meat at retail level. A high number of poultry farms are controlled by only

few firms, which provide animals, feedstuffs, technical and veteri- nary support to the farmers; in this productive system farmers are rewarded for their labour and the use of the farm structures (Cozzi and Ragno, 2003). The advantages of the integrated production system are the increased control of markets, the optimisation of poultry production through modern management strategies and technologies, the improvement of product quality and the reduc- tion of the transaction costs of exchanges at different stages of the value chain.

The Italian poultry sector, and in particular broiler production, is characterized by medium-high slaughtering weight of birds, among the highest in the EU according to van Horne and Bondt (2014). Basile (2015) reported that in 2014 more than 80% of broiler live weight (LW) slaughtered in Italy was covered by medium and heavy broilers (3 kg LW on average) and the average final LW of Italian broilers was 2.6 kg.

Intensive broiler production is dependent on concentrated feed derived from crop production, processing and transportation that take place mainly out of farm. As a consequence the upstream feed production processes are responsible for the main part of the environmental impacts associated with the broiler production chain (Bengtsson and Seddon, 2013; Katajajuuri, 2007; Pelletier, 2008). For this reason the Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) approach is the most suitable method for evaluating the environmental impact of poultry production. Several studies are available in the international literature on the assessment of the environmental impact of broiler meat production in intensive systems with a LCA methodology (Baumgartner et al., 2008; da Silva et al., 2014; González-García et al., 2014; Katajajuuri, 2007; Leinonen et al., 2012; Pelletier, 2008). However, most of the analyses are referred to lighter broilers, and cover only the chain from 'cradle to farm gate', excluding the downstream processes as slaughtering and packaging.

Productive performances (feed intake, feed conversion rate, live weight gain, mortality), manure management, energy costs and stocking density are identified as drivers which play an important role in the environmental impact of the broiler sector (Bengtsson and Seddon, 2013; da Silva et al., 2014; Leinonen et al., 2014).

Concerning stocking density, the Council Directive 2007/43/EC of the European Union, laying down minimum rules for the protection of chickens kept for meat production, fixes the maximum stocking density in the broiler farms that, at any time, must not exceed 33 kg/m2. Chickens could be kept at a higher stocking density (up to a maximum of 42 kg/m²) if additional conditions regarding management, monitoring, animal welfare and mortality can be guaranteed. Increasing stocking density can have a positive economic impact for industry, as revenues per unit of space increase linearly with density. Higher densities, however, may compromise poultry health and welfare; as a consequence, returns per unit of space in broiler production tend to plateau if density is excessive (Estevez, 2007). The relationship between stocking density and environmental impact of broiler production in a Life Cycle Assessment perspective was investi- gated in a few studies but only at low density levels (e.g., Leinonen et al., 2014).

The aim of the study was to evaluate the environmental impact of Italian broiler meat production in a vertically integrated system through a cradle to slaughterhouse gate Life Cycle approach. Considering the high final live weight of Italian broilers, the effect of slaughter weight on environmental impacts was assessed. More- over, the mitigation potential of the sector's Global Warming was evaluated in the perspective of the reduction of slaughtering weight. Lastly, a scenario analysis was performed to study the ef- fects of increasing stocking density on the environmental impact of broiler meat production.

Lastly, a scenario analysis was performed to study the ef- fects of increasing stocking density on the environmental impact of broiler meat production.
7: The world demand for animal products is increasing in response to growing population and income, especially in developing countries (Gerber et al., 2013). In particular, poultry meat repre- sents one of the most consumed animal food products in the world and the world poultry population is predicted to rise by over 90% between 2005 and 2050 (Alexandratos and Bruinsma, 2012).

The livestock sector significantly contributes to anthropogenic environmental impact through the emission of pollutants and greenhouse gases into soil, water and air and through the con- sumption of non-renewable and limited resources, such as water and land, mainly for the production of animal feeds.
In terms of greenhouse gas emission, animal productions are responsible for 14.5% of human-induced Global Warming Potential (GWP), expressed as CO2 equivalents; but poultry meat and egg

productions, despite their importance, contribute for just 8% to the livestock sector emissions (Gerber et al., 2013). Poultry meat, in fact, is one of the most environmentally efficient productions in com- parison to other animal meat products (Gerber et al., 2013; Nijdam et al., 2012; Williams et al., 2006), mainly due to the excellent feed conversion of poultry.

In 2014 in Italy approximately 1,261,000 t of poultry carcass weight was produced, over 69% of which represented by broiler meat (AVEC, 2015). With these figures, Italy is largely self-sufficient (107%) with regard to the supply of poultry meat. Most of the farms raising broilers are located in the Northern regions of Italy (Veneto, Emilia-Romagna and Lombardia) and are characterized by modern strategies of management and high production intensity (NOMISMA, 2016).

Most of the Italian poultry production (96%, according to NOMISMA, 2016) is characterized by a completely vertically inte- grated production system that includes all stages of the production process, from feed manufacturing to the delivery of poultry meat at retail level. A high number of poultry farms are controlled by only

few firms, which provide animals, feedstuffs, technical and veteri- nary support to the farmers; in this productive system farmers are rewarded for their labour and the use of the farm structures (Cozzi and Ragno, 2003). The advantages of the integrated production system are the increased control of markets, the optimisation of poultry production through modern management strategies and technologies, the improvement of product quality and the reduc- tion of the transaction costs of exchanges at different stages of the value chain.

The Italian poultry sector, and in particular broiler production, is characterized by medium-high slaughtering weight of birds, among the highest in the EU according to van Horne and Bondt (2014). Basile (2015) reported that in 2014 more than 80% of broiler live weight (LW) slaughtered in Italy was covered by medium and heavy broilers (3 kg LW on average) and the average final LW of Italian broilers was 2.6 kg.

Intensive broiler production is dependent on concentrated feed derived from crop production, processing and transportation that take place mainly out of farm. As a consequence the upstream feed production processes are responsible for the main part of the environmental impacts associated with the broiler production chain (Bengtsson and Seddon, 2013; Katajajuuri, 2007; Pelletier, 2008). For this reason the Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) approach is the most suitable method for evaluating the environmental impact of poultry production. Several studies are available in the international literature on the assessment of the environmental impact of broiler meat production in intensive systems with a LCA methodology (Baumgartner et al., 2008; da Silva et al., 2014; González-García et al., 2014; Katajajuuri, 2007; Leinonen et al., 2012; Pelletier, 2008). However, most of the analyses are referred to lighter broilers, and cover only the chain from 'cradle to farm gate', excluding the downstream processes as slaughtering and packaging.

Productive performances (feed intake, feed conversion rate, live weight gain, mortality), manure management, energy costs and stocking density are identified as drivers which play an important role in the environmental impact of the broiler sector (Bengtsson and Seddon, 2013; da Silva et al., 2014; Leinonen et al., 2014).

Concerning stocking density, the Council Directive 2007/43/EC of the European Union, laying down minimum rules for the protection of chickens kept for meat production, fixes the maximum stocking density in the broiler farms that, at any time, must not exceed 33 kg/m2. Chickens could be kept at a higher stocking density (up to a maximum of 42 kg/m²) if additional conditions regarding management, monitoring, animal welfare and mortality can be guaranteed. Increasing stocking density can have a positive economic impact for industry, as revenues per unit of space increase linearly with density. Higher densities, however, may compromise poultry health and welfare; as a consequence, returns per unit of space in broiler production tend to plateau if density is excessive (Estevez, 2007). The relationship between stocking density and environmental impact of broiler production in a Life Cycle Assessment perspective was investi- gated in a few studies but only at low density levels (e.g., Leinonen et al., 2014).

The aim of the study was to evaluate the environmental impact of Italian broiler meat production in a vertically integrated system through a cradle to slaughterhouse gate Life Cycle approach. Considering the high final live weight of Italian broilers, the effect of slaughter weight on environmental impacts was assessed. More- over, the mitigation potential of the sector's Global Warming was evaluated in the perspective of the reduction of slaughtering weight. Lastly, a scenario analysis was performed to study the ef- fects of increasing stocking density on the environmental impact of broiler meat production.
8: 2.1. Impacts of broiler meat production at slaughterhouse gate
9: The goal of the study was to apply the attributional LCA meth- odology in order to evaluate the environmental impact of broiler meat production in Italy in terms of Global Warming Potential, Acidification, Eutrophication, Terrestrial ecotoxicity potentials and Non-renewable fossil energy use. The functional unit was 1 kg of carcass weight (CW) at slaughterhouse gate.
10: Data were collected in a vertically integrated broiler group (Avicola Alimentare Monteverde S.r.l., Rovato, Italy) located in Lombardy, that produced in 2015 about 15 million of broilers, approximately 3% of the national slaughtered heads (Basile, 2015). According to the data of NOMISMA (2016), the studied group can be considered representative of the Italian broiler production system both in terms of vertically integrated organization and for its location (Northern Italy). The integrated system includes all stages of production: a feed industry, 9 breeder farms, a hatchery, 80 broiler fattening farms and a slaughterhouse. The broiler hybrids utilized were Ross 308. The details of production parameters, ob- tained through personal interviews to the owners of the broiler group, are showed in Table 1.

One-day-old parent birds, provided by a breeding company located in Germany, were reared in the breeder farms on litter. Commercial broilers were reared on sawdust as litter in environ- mentally controlled houses. The animals had free access to water and commercial pelleted diets, formulated to meet nutritional re- quirements for all animal categories. The day-old hybrid broiler chicks were grown to appropriate market age and weight. In particular, broilers (Table 1) were slaughtered at 32 days with a LW of 1.6 kg (light broilers), at 40 days with a LW of 2.5 kg (medium broilers) and at 53 days with a LW of 3.8 kg (roasters, i.e. heavy broilers).

Animal rearing followed the indication of European Directive 2007/43/EC that specifies the rules for the protection of chickens reared for meat production, providing the minimal standards required. In particular, the final stocking density applied was 25 kg/ m² for pullets, 28 kg/m2 for breeders and 33 kg/m2 for broiler production (Table 1).
The slaughterhouse produced rotisserie chickens from light birds, chicken parts from medium broilers and whole chickens from roasters.

The majority of feeding ingredients (Table 2) came from Euro- pean countries, whereas protein feeds, mainly soybean meal, originated from Brazil. All the information regarding activities at the slaughterhouse, in particular energy consumptions, were considered. The packaging phase was included in the system boundaries; in particular pack- aging materials were expandable polystyrene (EPS), packaging film, corrugate boxes and craft paper.

Primary data were collected through personal interviews to the owners to gather information about: production performances (feed intake, feed conversion rate, live weight gain, mortality), diet composition (ingredients, protein, energy), housing system, manure storage and management, external inputs (i.e: feeds, fer- tilizers, bedding materials, etc) and energy consumption (hatchery, breeder rearing, broiler fattening and slaughterhouse).

The environmental impacts were not allocated but they were entirely attributed to chicken meat, the unique product of the vertically integrated system. Wastes from slaughterhouse addressed to pet food (neck, head, blood, skin and viscera), part of manure (destined to compost) and unfertile eggs were not considered because of their negligible economic value. The weight of broilers dead during the fattening cycle was excluded from the total live weight at farm gate.

Manure methane emission was calculated using the equation proposed by IPCC (2006) and LEAP (2014). Volatile solid excretion was predicted using feed intake obtained from the interviews, fixed diet digestibility (80%) and ash content in the manure (15%; Bolan et al., 2010) using the formula no. 5 from LEAP (2014). The conver- sion factor for solid storage was 0.67 for all categories (IPCC, 2006).

Nitrogen (N) excretion was calculated starting from dry matter intake, protein content of the ration and nitrogen retained by animals following indication of LEAP (2014), which suggested values of N retained of 0.602 for broilers and 0.588 for hens. Emission of N20 from manure management was calculated considering the equation of TIER 2 of IPCC (2006) for solid storage and 0.001 as emission factor.

Ammonia and nitrous oxide emissions from animal housing and manure management were calculated using the TIER 2 equations of EEA (2009) on the basis of total N excreted by animals. The N emission factors, as a proportion of total ammonia nitrogen, were specified for solid manure and each step of manure handling. Also N content of bedding material was considered.

Ammonia and NO emitted during manure spreading and urea application (100 kg N/ha) on maize were estimated according to EAA guidelines (2009). The amount of N leached was estimated following the IPCC (2006) model. To estimate emissions of PO43, the amount of phosphorus lost in dissolved form to surface water (run-off) and leached was considered as proposed by Nemecek and Kagi (2007), considering P excretion of each animal (0.26 g/d).

Carbon dioxide (CO2) emissions from fuel combustion were estimated on the basis of fuel consumption declared in all processes of the vertically integrated farming system. Emissions occurring during field operations for maize cultivation were estimated modifying the processes of the Ecoinvent (2013) database. Emis- sions from livestock respiration and the variation in soil carbon stocks were not taken into consideration.

The emissions related to the production of commercial feed materials (from crop growing to feed factory processing), bedding material, chemical fertilizers, pesticides, diesel and electricity used in the integrated farming system were quantified mainly using data from Ecoinvent V3 (2013) and Agri-footprint (Blonk Consultants, 2014) database.

Manure methane emission was calculated using the equation proposed by IPCC (2006) and LEAP (2014). Ammonia and nitrous oxide emissions from animal housing and manure management were calculated using the TIER 2 equations of EEA (2009) on the basis of total N excreted by animals. The N emission factors, as a proportion of total ammonia nitrogen, were specified for solid manure and each step of manure handling. Also N content of bedding material was considered.

Ammonia and NO emitted during manure spreading and urea application (100 kg N/ha) on maize were estimated according to EAA guidelines (2009). The amount of N leached was estimated following the IPCC (2006) model. To estimate emissions of PO43, the amount of phosphorus lost in dissolved form to surface water (run-off) and leached was considered as proposed by Nemecek and Kagi (2007), considering P excretion of each animal (0.26 g/d).

Carbon dioxide (CO2) emissions from fuel combustion were estimated on the basis of fuel consumption declared in all processes of the vertically integrated farming system. Emissions occurring during field operations for maize cultivation were estimated modifying the processes of the Ecoinvent (2013) database. Emis- sions from livestock respiration and the variation in soil carbon stocks were not taken into consideration.

Manure methane emission was calculated using the equation proposed by IPCC (2006) and LEAP (2014). Volatile solid excretion was predicted using feed intake obtained from the interviews, fixed diet digestibility (80%) and ash content in the manure (15%; Bolan et al., 2010) using the formula no. 5 from LEAP (2014). The conver- sion factor for solid storage was 0.67 for all categories (IPCC, 2006).

Nitrogen (N) excretion was calculated starting from dry matter intake, protein content of the ration and nitrogen retained by animals following indication of LEAP (2014), which suggested values of N retained of 0.602 for broilers and 0.588 for hens. Emission of N20 from manure management was calculated considering the equation of TIER 2 of IPCC (2006) for solid storage and 0.001 as emission factor.

Ammonia and nitrous oxide emissions from animal housing and manure management were calculated using the TIER 2 equations of EEA (2009) on the basis of total N excreted by animals. The N emission factors, as a proportion of total ammonia nitrogen, were specified for solid manure and each step of manure handling. Also N content of bedding material was considered.

Ammonia and NO emitted during manure spreading and urea application (100 kg N/ha) on maize were estimated according to EAA guidelines (2009). The amount of N leached was estimated following the IPCC (2006) model. To estimate emissions of PO43, the amount of phosphorus lost in dissolved form to surface water (run-off) and leached was considered as proposed by Nemecek and Kagi (2007), considering P excretion of each animal (0.26 g/d).

Carbon dioxide (CO2) emissions from fuel combustion were estimated on the basis of fuel consumption declared in all processes of the vertically integrated farming system. Emissions occurring during field operations for maize cultivation were estimated modifying the processes of the Ecoinvent (2013) database. Emis- sions from livestock respiration and the variation in soil carbon stocks were not taken into consideration.

The emissions related to the production of commercial feed materials (from crop growing to feed factory processing), bedding material, chemical fertilizers, pesticides, diesel and electricity used in the integrated farming system were quantified mainly using data from Ecoinvent V3 (2013) and Agri-footprint (Blonk Consultants, 2014) database.

The system boundaries included the entire life cycle of broilers, the upstream and the downstream processes (from parents rearing to slaughterhouse and packaging), using 1 kg Carcass Weight (CW) as functional unit as suggested by LEAP (2014) for the LCA assess- ments at the stage of leaving the meat processing plants. All direct and indirect inputs and emissions arising from the production and processing of feeds, energy sources, bedding and packaging mate- rials were considered. According to Pelletier et al. (2014), produc- tion and maintenance of machinery and buildings were not included. Similarly, animal drugs and the production of one-day- old chicks entering the breeder farms were not considered in the assessment. Details of the system boundaries are presented in Fig. 1.

The majority of feeding ingredients (Table 2) came from Euro- pean countries, whereas protein feeds, mainly soybean meal, originated from Brazil. All the information regarding activities at the slaughterhouse, in particular energy consumptions, were considered. The packaging phase was included in the system boundaries; in particular pack- aging materials were expandable polystyrene (EPS), packaging film, corrugate boxes and craft paper.

Primary data were collected through personal interviews to the owners to gather information about: production performances (feed intake, feed conversion rate, live weight gain, mortality), diet composition (ingredients, protein, energy), housing system, manure storage and management, external inputs (i.e: feeds, fer- tilizers, bedding materials, etc) and energy consumption (hatchery, breeder rearing, broiler fattening and slaughterhouse).

The environmental impacts were not allocated but they were entirely attributed to chicken meat, the unique product of the vertically integrated system. Wastes from slaughterhouse addressed to pet food (neck, head, blood, skin and viscera), part of manure (destined to compost) and unfertile eggs were not considered because of their negligible economic value. The weight of broilers dead during the fattening cycle was excluded from the total live weight at farm gate.

The number of animals reared was calculated as average annual population as suggested by IPCC (2006), considering the following lifetime cycles: 154 d for female chicks before the starting of ovo- deposition, 294 d for breeders, 32 d for light broilers, 40 d for medium broilers and 53 for roasters (Table 2). In order to compare the impacts originated from the different final LW categories of broilers a separate impact assessment per kg of LW was performed limiting the system boundaries to broiler fattening farm gate excluding breeder rearing, hatchery, slaugh- terhouse and packaging.
2.3. Mitigation potential of the Italian sector's global warming through the reduction of final broiler live weight
To quantify the mitigation potential of Global Warming of the Italian broiler meat sector achievable through the reduction of the
slaughter LW of broilers, the GWP obtained for the category of light broilers was applied to the entire Italian broiler production.

The aim of the scenario analysis was to study the effects of increasing stocking density on the environmental impact of broiler meat production. Following the indications of European Directive 2007/43/EC, a stocking density of 42 kg/m2 in the fattening phase was applied. The higher stocking density was assumed only for roasters, the most impactful and most represented broiler category in the system, whereas for the other weight categories (light and medium broilers) stocking density was not modified. Rearing period was assumed as the same of the baseline scenario and, following the indication of Dozier et al. (2005, 2006), dressing percentage and mortality were supposed to be equal to the baseline scenario. Conversely, according to Dozier et al. (2005, 2006) a decrease of feed intake (-3.5 g/head), a slight worsening of feed conversion ratio (+1.4%) and a decrease of the average final live weight of roasters (-0.13 kg/head), in comparison to the baseline scenario, were assumed.

23: <header>3.1. Impacts of broiler meat production at slaughterhouse gate </header>

Environmental impacts of the entire integrated broiler produc- tion system at slaughterhouse gate (including packaging) per kg of carcass weight are showed in Table 3. According to other studies (Bengtsson and Seddon, 2013; Katajajuuri, 2007; Pelletier, 2008), the broiler fattening, and the related feed production, was the phase that gave the most important contribution to the environ- mental impact of the broiler production, followed by the breeder compartment.

The average GWP of the broiler production system, including slaughtering and packaging, was 5.52 kg CO2 eq. per kg of carcass weight. In the international literature GWP values per kg of chicken carcass weight vary approximately from 2.5 to 4.4 kg CO2 eq. (Bengtsson and Seddon, 2013; da Silva et al., 2014; González-García et al., 2014; Leinonen et al., 2012). However, in some studies slaughtering and packaging burdens were not considered (Leinonen et al., 2012) and the effect of land use change for soybean production was generally omitted or not mentioned. Moreover, most of the previous analyses were focused on the production of light-medium broilers: in these studies the average live weight at slaughtering ranged from 1.9 to 2.5 and the duration of the fattening cycle from 31 to 42 days. In the present study heavy broilers (roasters) represented approximately 44% of slaughtered animals and their average slaughtering LW was very high, approximately 3.8 kg. The worse Feed Conversion Ratio (FCR) of heavy broilers in comparison with light and medium ones is probably the main cause of the high GWP per kg of carcass weight of the Italian broiler.

In accordance with the findings of other authors (Bengtsson and Seddon, 2013; da Silva et al., 2014), the contributions of hatchery and slaughterhouse compartments to GWP/kg CW were very low and mostly related to electricity consumption.

Acidification was significantly lower than that reported by da Silva et al. (2014) and Leinonen et al. (2012, 2014). In particular, da Silva et al. (2014) pointed out that, in the scientific literature, large ranges of acidification are reported, mainly because of different methods used for the estimation of nitrogen and phos- phorous emissions; due to these differences, comparisons are difficult. However, the main contribution, also for the acidification category, was represented by the production of purchased feeds.

Eutrophication potential per kg of CW was similar to those re- ported by da Silva et al. (2014) and Leinonen et al. (2012, 2014). Terrestrial ecotoxicity was 9.56 g 1,4 DCB eq. per kg CW, in line with the result obtained by da Silva et al. (2014). Breeder farming, hatchery and slaughterhouse/packaging compartments contrib- uted significantly to the impact category of terrestrial ecotoxicity, with 4.62%, 3.34% and 18.6%, respectively, of total impact.

The total Non-renewable fossil energy use was slightly lower than the results of Leinonen et al. (2012, 2014). Hatchery and slaughterhouse gave important contributions to Non-renewable fossil energy consumption: 1.55% for hatchery and 11.1% for slaughterhouse. In particular, the latter is similar to the result ob- tained by Thevenot et al. (2013).

On the basis of impact potentials per kg of carcass weight, broiler production is confirmed to be a low impact meat produc- tion, even if the Italian broiler seems to be more impactful of broilers reared in other countries. According to De Vries and De Boer (2010) and Nijdam et al. (2012), for climate change, energy use and land use, the production of 1 kg of beef protein has the highest impact, followed by pork, whereas chicken has the lowest impact. Similar conclusions were recently reported by Zucali et al. (2016) with specific reference to the GWP of livestock sector in Lombardy.
3.2. Impacts of broilers of different live weight at fattening farm gate
A separate impact assessment per kg of live weight was per- formed limiting the system boundaries to broiler fattening farm gate, excluding breeder rearing, hatchery, slaughterhouse and packaging.

Roaster was the most impactful chicken production with respect to all the impacts evaluated (Table This result is due to the longer production cycle (roasters were slaughtered at 53 d, whereas light and medium broilers at 32 and 40 d, respectively) and, conse- quently, to the worsening, with ageing, of FCR, expressed as g feed intake per g weight gain (1.88 for roasters vs 1.63 and 1.50, for medium and light broilers, respectively; Table 1). Other authors observed a reduction of feed efficiency as broiler slaughtering age increased (Castellini et al., 2012). Nevertheless, the results for GWP of all broiler categories, including roasters, fall in the range between
2.0 and 5.5 kg CO2 eq/kg LW, reported by Bengtsson and Seddon (2013) for LCA studies from cradle to farm gate.

The result of GWP for medium broilers was very close to the value reported by Baumgartner et al. (2008) on chicken of the same life cycle duration (3.31 kg CO2 eq./kg LW at farm gate; 41 d) with ration based on soybean meal from Brazil.

Global warming potential reported by da Silva et al. (2014) for 1.92 kg of LW broiler reared in standard intensive conditions in France (2.22 kg CO2 eq./kg LW at farm gate) was lower than those obtained for light and medium broilers in this study probably due the fact that in the French scenario they assumed that soybean production was not associated with any deforestation-related emissions. Conversely, for acidification and eutrophication poten- tials, the results found by da Silva et al. (2014) for French standard intensive scenario were higher because of worse feed conversion ratio, despite the similar rearing period.

Feed production gave the most important contribution to all environmental categories in the broiler fattening (Fig. 2); in particular production of purchased feeds (both energy and protein feeds) accounted for 92% of GWP and 76% of acidification potential. Similarly, Pelletier (2008) found a very important contribution of feed provision on all impact categories considered in his study. Analysing the environmental performances along the US broiler poultry chain, the study showed that the feed production phase accounted for 80% of the energy use, 82% of greenhouse gas emissions, 98% of ozone depleting emissions, 96% of acidifying emissions, and 97% of eutrophying emissions associated with the cradle-to-farm gate production of broiler. Nguyen et al. (2012) re- ported that environmental impacts of poultry feed increased with energy and protein content of the formula but feeds with high energy and protein contents tend to result in better feed conversion ratios.

Among the purchased feeds, protein feeds (especially soybean meal) gave the most important contributions to the environmental impacts: from 42 to 79% of total impact in the five categories considered. The high contribution to environmental impact of soy products was due to land use change and transport. For fishmeal the high environmental impact was due to the fuel inputs for fishing and the production of fishmeal, as pointed out by Pelletier (2008). According to Baumgartner et al. In accordance with the findings of other authors (Bengtsson and Seddon, 2013; da Silva et al., 2014), the contributions of hatchery and slaughterhouse compartments to GWP/kg CW were very low and mostly related to electricity consumption.

(2014) pointed out that, in the scientific literature, large ranges of acidification are reported, mainly because of different methods used for the estimation of nitrogen and phos- phorous emissions; due to these differences, comparisons are difficult. Roaster was the most impactful chicken production with respect to all the impacts evaluated (Table This result is due to the longer production cycle (roasters were slaughtered at 53 d, whereas light and medium broilers at 32 and 40 d, respectively) and, conse- quently, to the worsening, with ageing, of FCR, expressed as g feed intake per g weight gain (1.88 for roasters vs 1.63 and 1.50, for medium and light broilers, respectively; Table 1). 1: <header>3.3. Mitigation potential of the Italian sector's global warming through the reduction of final broiler live weight </header>

Starting from the results obtained for broiler production in the integrated system, a quantification of mitigation potential of GWP of chicken production at national scale by reducing slaughtering weight of broilers was performed.

According to Basile (2015) in 2014 the Italian broiler production was 1248 Mt of live weight; 18.7% of slaughtered birds were light broilers (1.6 kg LW on average) and 81.3% medium and heavy broilers (3.0 kg LW on average). The average final LW of Italian broilers was 2.6 kg. Adopting the GWP values per kg LW obtained from the system studied (including also breeding and hatchery phases) the total GWP of the Italian broiler sector at fattening farm gate is 4.33 Mt CO2 eq.

In the hypothesis that the entire broiler LW production in Italy was obtained from light broilers (1.6 kg LW), the total GWP for the Italian broiler sector would be reduced by about 12% (3.81 Mt CO2 eq.) in comparison with the current situation. A final LW of 1.6 kg is common in tropical areas (Thevenot et al., 2013) and represents approximately 30% of the US broiler market (Dozier et al., 2006). In most European countries, the slaughter LW of broiler is between 2 and 2.5 kg. Exceptions are France with a slightly lower LW (1.9 kg) and Spain with a LW of 2.7 kg (van Horne and Bondt, 2014). Conversely, in Italy light broilers cover only a small part of total slaughtered broiler LW (18.7%) and according to the figures of Basile (2015), the average final LW of Italian broilers is 2.6 kg on average. The results suggest that reducing life cycle duration and final LW

of broilers could have a mitigation potential in terms of greenhouse gas emission of the Italian broiler industry. This solution could permit to optimize the productive efficiency with an improvement of feed conversion ratio.

3: <header>3.4. High stocking density scenario analysis </header>

A scenario analysis was performed to assess the effects of increasing stocking density up to 42 kg/m² final live weight, the maximum density allowed by the European legislation, on envi- ronmental impact of broiler production in the integrated farming system. The different stocking density was applied only on roaster production, the most impactful and most represented broiler category in the studied system. In Table 5 both the impacts of the sole roaster production at fattening farm gate on kg LW and of the whole system at slaughterhouse gate on kg CW are reported.

As a consequence of the increased stocking density, the number of heavy roasters produced in the integrated broiler system could rise from 6,399,360 to 8,144,640 and the total meat production could increase from 27,445 t to 31,242

The studies of Dozier et al. (2005, 2006) reported that increased stocking density, above 25-30 kg/m 2 results in decreased weight gain, reduced feed intake, and worsened feed conversion ratio. Accordingly to these studies, in the scenario analysis a decrease of feed intake, a slight worsening of feed conversion ratio and a decrease of the average final live weight of roaster, in comparison to the baseline scenario, were assumed.

As a result the increased density caused slightly higher values (5%, on average) of all impact categories for roaster production per kg LW at fattening farm gate in comparison with the baseline sce- nario (Table 5).

Considering the entire integrated broiler system, the high stocking density scenario determined a slight increase of GWP, Acidification and Eutrophication per kg CW at slaughterhouse gate (+3%, on average) but did not affect the other two impact categories (Terrestrial ecotoxicity and Non-renewable fossil energy) probably because the increased production reduced the energy expenditure

The results obtained from this scenario analysis showed that the increase in stocking density up to the maximum allowed by the European legislation increased the total meat production of the integrated broiler system but did not significantly modify the environmental impact per kg CW. This means that the intensifica- tion, even if producing higher yields per unit area, and probably higher farm revenues, does not lead to advantages in terms of unitary environmental impacts. Moreover some possible disad- vantages of high densities in terms of animal welfare have to be considered. Several authors have reported worsening of health conditions and animal welfare by increasing bird density (Estevez, 2007). Conversely Dawkins et al. (2004) in a large experiment concluded that chicken welfare is influenced more by housing conditions than by stocking density.

The results obtained from this scenario analysis showed that the increase in stocking density up to the maximum allowed by the European legislation increased the total meat production of the integrated broiler system but did not significantly modify the environmental impact per kg CW. This means that the intensifica- tion, even if producing higher yields per unit area, and probably higher farm revenues, does not lead to advantages in terms of unitary environmental impacts. Moreover some possible disad- vantages of high densities in terms of animal welfare have to be considered. Several authors have reported worsening of health conditions and animal welfare by increasing bird density (Estevez, 2007). Conversely Dawkins et al. (2004) in a large experiment concluded that chicken welfare is influenced more by housing conditions than by stocking density.
5: <header>3.4. High stocking density scenario analysis </header>

A scenario analysis was performed to assess the effects of increasing stocking density up to 42 kg/m² final live weight, the maximum density allowed by the European legislation, on envi- ronmental impact of broiler production in the integrated farming system. The different stocking density was applied only on roaster production, the most impactful and most represented broiler category in the studied system. In Table 5 both the impacts of the sole roaster production at fattening farm gate on kg LW and of the whole system at slaughterhouse gate on kg CW are reported.

As a consequence of the increased stocking density, the number of heavy roasters produced in the integrated broiler system could rise from 6,399,360 to 8,144,640 and the total meat production could increase from 27,445 t to 31,242

The studies of Dozier et al. (2005, 2006) reported that increased stocking density, above 25-30 kg/m 2 results in decreased weight gain, reduced feed intake, and worsened feed conversion ratio. Accordingly to these studies, in the scenario analysis a decrease of feed intake, a slight worsening of feed conversion ratio and a decrease of the average final live weight of roaster, in comparison to the baseline scenario, were assumed.

As a result the increased density caused slightly higher values (5%, on average) of all impact categories for roaster production per kg LW at fattening farm gate in comparison with the baseline sce- nario (Table 5).

Considering the entire integrated broiler system, the high stocking density scenario determined a slight increase of GWP, Acidification and Eutrophication per kg CW at slaughterhouse gate (+3%, on average) but did not affect the other two impact categories (Terrestrial ecotoxicity and Non-renewable fossil energy) probably because the increased production reduced the energy expenditure

7: In conclusion, this study confirmed that the production of broiler in Italy is more impactful than in other countries, mainly due to the high age and weight at slaughter. Nevertheless in com- parison to other meat productions, broiler meat has lower unitary environmental impacts. This result could suggest the opportunity of national or supranational policy interventions to promote pro- duction and consumption of poultry meat, instead of beef and pork, to reduce GHG emissions from livestock productions; however care is needed because different meat products have different nutri- tional properties and fill different roles for consumers.

Along the production chain, broiler fattening, and the related feed production, is the most impactful phase; on the contrary hatchery, slaughterhouse and packaging impacted slightly, espe- cially in terms of Global Warming, Acidification and Eutrophication per kg of carcass weight. Heavy broilers (roasters) showed the higher impacts per kg of live weight compared to light and medium broilers mainly due to the worse feed conversion ratio. The main contribution to environmental impacts in the fattening phase was represented by feed production and processing; in particular pur- chased protein feeds, especially soybean meal, significantly affected global warming potential. For this reason, it could be interesting to evaluate the partial substitution of soybean meal with other protein sources although the high protein and amino acid requirements of modern broiler hybrids make difficult to implement this strategy. The results confirmed that feed production, type of protein feed, feed conversion ratio and slaughter weight have a strong influence on the environmental impact of broiler production. One of the strategy to decrease the environmental impact of the Italian broiler sector could be the reduction of slaughter weight and age in order to improve feed efficiency. This strategy, applied at national level, could reduce the global warming potential of the broiler industry by approximately 12%. However, at the moment, the major
limitation is represented by the Italian market demand for heavy broilers.

The intensification of broiler production through increased stocking density up to the maximum allowed did not lead to a significant reduction of the unitary environmental impacts, mainly because of the worsening feed conversion ratio. Moreover some possible disadvantages of high densities in terms of animal welfare have to be considered.
